title: Per capita CO₂ emissions from international commercial passenger flights, tourism-adjusted
subtitle: International aviation emissions are allocated to the country of departure, then [adjusted for tourism](#dod:tourism-adjusted).
caption:
Per capita CO₂ emissions from international commercial passenger flights, tourism-adjusted

International aviation emissions are allocated to the country of departure, then adjusted for tourism.

This choropleth world map displays 2024 per capita CO₂ emissions from international commercial passenger flights, adjusted for tourism, with values expressed in kilograms. The legend ranges from 0 kg to 1,000 kg, marked at intervals of 10, 20, 50, 100, 200, and 500 kg, with the darkest red indicating the highest emissions. Canada and Iceland fall into the darkest visible band, exceeding 500 kg per capita. The United States, Norway, Sweden, Finland, and New Zealand appear in the next darkest shade, placing them in the 200–500 kg range. Across Western and Northern Europe—including the United Kingdom, Germany, France, and Spain—countries sit in mid-range orange shades, generally between 50 and 200 kg. In Asia, Japan and South Korea are in similar mid-range bands, while China and India appear in lighter shades below 50 kg. Most of Latin America, Africa, and Southeast Asia are colored in the lightest shades or show hatching, indicating either very low emissions or no available data. Australia is hatched, as are large portions of Central Asia, the Middle East outside the Gulf states, and nearly all of Africa. The United Arab Emirates and Qatar stand out in the Middle East with darker orange coloring, suggesting values above 100 kg. The visual distribution shows the highest per capita emissions concentrated in North America, Northern Europe, and Oceania, with progressively lighter shades across Asia, Latin America, and most of the Global South.
